metadata.title: Supply and Demand
metadata.subtitle: Understanding the Market Forces That Set Every Price

content.title: Supply and Demand
content.subtitle: Understanding the Market Forces That Set Every Price

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
In {{STATE_NAME}}, median rent is ${{STATE_MEDIAN_RENT}}/monthŌĆösignificantly different from the national average of $1,400. These price differences aren't random; they reflect fundamental supply and demand forces at work in every market.

Why does rent cost ${{STATE_MEDIAN_RENT}}/month in {{STATE_NAME}}?
And why do some prices change daily while others stay stable?

slides.notes:
Discussion prompts:
ŌĆó "Can you think of other examples where prices change predictably?"
ŌĆó Preview that supply and demand explains virtually ALL price changes.

objectives.description: Describe the law of demand and law of supply and how they work together to determine market prices.
Examine how supply and demand curves interact to create market equilibrium prices in {{STATE_NAME}}.
Recognize factors that cause supply and demand curves to shift in real markets like housing and labor.
Use supply and demand analysis to make smarter personal finance decisions about timing purchases and careers.

terms.definition: The quantity of a good or service that consumers are willing and able to buy at various prices.
terms.example: At $1,500/month rent, 80,000 people want apartments in {{STATE_NAME}}. At $2,500, only 50,000 do.

terms.definition: The quantity of a good or service that producers are willing and able to offer at various prices.
terms.example: At $2,500/month rent, developers build 100,000 units. At $1,500, they only build 60,000.

terms.definition: The price point where quantity demanded equals quantity suppliedŌĆöthe market "clears" with no shortages or surpluses.
terms.example: At ${{STATE_MEDIAN_RENT}}/month, the number of renters equals available apartments in {{STATE_NAME}}.

terms.definition: When the entire supply or demand curve moves due to factors other than price (income, preferences, costs).
terms.example: {{STATE_NAME}} population grows ŌåÆ demand curve shifts RIGHT ŌåÆ equilibrium price rises.

leftPanel.title: Price Goes Up ŌåÆ Quantity Demanded Goes Down
paragraphs: Why does this happen? Three key reasons:
1. Substitution Effect: When prices rise, people switch to cheaper alternatives (beef ŌåÆ chicken, driving ŌåÆ public transit).
2. Income Effect: Higher prices reduce your purchasing powerŌĆöyou effectively have less money.
3. Diminishing Utility: The 5th pizza slice isn't as satisfying as the 1stŌĆöyou'll only buy more at lower prices.

highlightBox.text: Ē│ē The demand curve always slopes DOWNWARD: Higher price = fewer buyers.

infoCard.title: Your Personal Demand Curve:
items: $10/cup coffee ŌåÆ Once a month
$5/cup coffee ŌåÆ Few times a week
$2/cup coffee ŌåÆ Every day
You have a demand curve for everything!

leftPanel.title: Price Goes Up ŌåÆ Quantity Supplied Goes Up
paragraphs: Why does this happen? Producers respond to incentives:
1. Profit Motive: Higher prices = higher profits ŌåÆ businesses produce more to capture that profit.
2. New Entrants: High prices attract new producers to enter the market (more builders, more sellers).
3. Opportunity Cost: Higher prices justify using more expensive production methods that weren't profitable before.

highlightBox.text: Ē│ł The supply curve always slopes UPWARD: Higher price = more producers willing to sell.

infoCard.title: Supply in {{STATE_NAME}}:
items: High home prices ŌåÆ More construction
High wages ŌåÆ More workers enter field
High rents ŌåÆ More landlords rent units
Prices signal where resources should go

content.headerTitle: Market Equilibrium: Where Supply Meets Demand

leftPanel.title: The Price Where Markets Clear
paragraphs: At equilibrium, quantity demanded = quantity supplied. No shortages, no surpluses.
Above equilibrium: Too many sellers, not enough buyers ŌåÆ prices fall (surplus)
Below equilibrium: Too many buyers, not enough sellers ŌåÆ prices rise (shortage)
Markets naturally move toward equilibrium through the price mechanismŌĆöno central planner needed!

highlightBox.text: ŌÜ¢’ĖÅ Equilibrium is where the supply and demand curves INTERSECT.

infoCard.title: {{STATE_NAME}} Housing Market:
items: Low inventory = High demand vs. low supply
Prices rising toward new equilibrium
Buyers compete = bidding wars
6 months inventory = balanced market

layoutData.title: Factors That Move the Entire Demand Curve
paragraphs: When something OTHER than price changes, the whole demand curve shifts left or right:
bulletPoints: Income Changes: Rising incomes ŌåÆ demand increases (curve shifts RIGHT)
Population Growth: More buyers in {{STATE_NAME}} ŌåÆ demand shifts RIGHT
Tastes & Preferences: Trends, health concerns, or viral products shift demand
Price of Substitutes: Gas prices rise ŌåÆ demand for EVs increases
Price of Complements: Coffee prices fall ŌåÆ demand for creamer rises
Future Expectations: Expect prices to rise ŌåÆ buy now (demand shifts RIGHT)

keyPoint.text: ­¤ōŖ {{STATE_NAME}} population: {{STATE_POPULATION}} with {{STATE_JOB_GROWTH}}% job growth ŌåÆ Demand for housing keeps shifting RIGHT.

layoutData.title: Factors That Move the Entire Supply Curve
paragraphs: When production costs or conditions change, the whole supply curve shifts:
bulletPoints: Input Costs: Lumber prices rise ŌåÆ supply of new homes decreases (shifts LEFT)
Technology: Better tech ŌåÆ can produce more efficiently (shifts RIGHT)
Number of Sellers: New builders enter {{STATE_NAME}} market ŌåÆ supply increases
Government Policy: Taxes decrease supply; subsidies increase it
Natural Events: Drought, pandemic, disasters reduce supply (shifts LEFT)
Expectations: Expect higher prices ŌåÆ hold back supply now

keyPoint.text: ­¤ÅŚ’ĖÅ In {{STATE_NAME}}: Rising construction costs + zoning limits ŌåÆ Supply can't keep up with demand ŌåÆ Prices rise.

leftPanel.title: Economic Concept
leftPanel.content: Price Ceilings set a maximum price landlords can charge.

rightPanel.title: Real World Context
rightPanel.content: In {{STATE_NAME}}, the median rent is currently ${{STATE_MEDIAN_RENT}}/mo.

slides.notes:
Common Misconceptions:

| Misconception | Clarification |
|--------------|---------------|
| "High prices in {{STATE_NAME}} are just because of greed." | Prices reflect supply and demand balance. {{STATE_NAME}}'s home price of ${{STATE_MEDIAN_HOME_PRICE}} reflects its specific combination of demand factors (population growth: {{STATE_POPULATION_GROWTH}}, job growth: {{STATE_JOB_GROWTH}}) and supply factors (construction trends, land availability). |
| "Supply and demand only applies to goods, not services or labor." | Supply and demand applies to EVERYTHING with a priceŌĆöincluding wages ({{STATE_NAME}}'s {{STATE_UNEMPLOYMENT_RATE}}% unemployment reflects labor market balance), rent ({{STATE_NAME}}'s ${{STATE_MEDIAN_RENT}}/mo), interest rates, and stock prices. |
| "If prices are high, they'll stay high forever." | High prices attract new supply (builders construct more housing, workers enter high-paying fields). Over time, increased supply puts downward pressure on prices. Markets self-correctŌĆöit's a question of how quickly. |
| "The government controls all prices." | While regulations affect some prices (minimum wage, rent control), most prices are determined by millions of individual supply and demand decisions. Government can shift supply or demand curves but rarely overrides market forces long-term. |

content.question: Looking at {{STATE_NAME}}'s dataŌĆöpopulation {{STATE_POPULATION}}, job growth {{STATE_JOB_GROWTH}}%, and {{STATE_HOUSING_INVENTORY}} months housing inventoryŌĆöwhat would need to change for home prices to decrease?

leftPanel.title: Time Your Purchases Strategically
paragraphs: Buy when supply is HIGH and demand is LOW:
­¤ÜŚ Cars: End of model year (dealers clearing inventory) or winter months (low demand)
Ō£ł’ĖÅ Travel: Tuesday/Wednesday flights (low demand) instead of weekends
­¤ÅĀ Housing: Winter months typically have fewer buyers competing
­¤æĢ Clothing: Off-season purchases (winter coats in spring)

highlightBox.text: Ē▓Ī In {{STATE_NAME}}, home prices are typically lowest in winter when fewer people are house-hunting.

infoCard.title: Career Planning with Supply/Demand:
items: High demand + low supply = rising wages
Healthcare in {{STATE_NAME}}: High demand
Tech: Digital transformation driving demand
Skilled trades: Limited supply as workers retire

leftPanel.title: Your Skills Are a Product in the Labor Market
paragraphs: The labor market works just like any other market:
Employers = Demand for workers with specific skills
Workers = Supply of those skills
When demand for your skills exceeds supply, your wages rise!
With {{STATE_NAME}}'s unemployment rate at {{STATE_UNEMPLOYMENT_RATE}}%, the labor market is tightŌĆöemployers are competing for workers.

highlightBox.text: ĒŠ» Choose careers where demand is rising and supply is limited for maximum earning potential.

infoCard.title: High Demand Careers:
items: Healthcare (aging population)
Technology (digital transformation)
Skilled trades (retiring workforce)
Renewable energy (policy shift)

content.question:
What skills do you have (or could develop) where demand is high and supply is limited?

How can you use supply and demand thinking to maximize your earning potential?

leftColumn.title: ŌØī Don't Do This
items: Buy when demand peaks (holidays, summer)
Panic buy during shortages (prices highest)
Ignore market signals when choosing careers
Think prices are "unfair" rather than signals
Wait to buy if demand is clearly rising

rightColumn.title: Ō£ō Do This Instead
items: Buy during off-peak times (winter, weekdays)
Wait for supply to catch up after disruptions
Research supply/demand for career fields
Use prices as information to guide decisions
Act quickly in markets with rising demand

takeaways.title: Law of Demand
takeaways.description: Price Ōåæ ŌåÆ Quantity Demanded Ōåō. Higher prices mean fewer buyers (and vice versa).

takeaways.title: Law of Supply
takeaways.description: Price Ōåæ ŌåÆ Quantity Supplied Ōåæ. Higher prices attract more producers and sellers.

takeaways.title: Markets Self-Adjust
takeaways.description: Prices rise to eliminate shortages and fall to eliminate surplusesŌĆöreaching equilibrium.

takeaways.title: Prices Are Signals
takeaways.description: High prices signal scarcity and opportunity. Use this knowledge for timing purchases and careers.

questions.question: If {{STATE_NAME}}'s population grows by 500,000 people, what happens to the demand curve for housing? What happens to equilibrium prices?
Why do home prices continue rising when median price is already ${{STATE_MEDIAN_HOME_PRICE}}? Use supply and demand to explain.
Gas prices are higher in summer than winter. Is this a demand shift or a supply shift? Explain.
How can understanding supply and demand help you choose a career that pays well?

slides.notes:
Answer Key:

1. Population growth of 500,000 people shifts the demand curve for housing to the RIGHT (more buyers at every price point). With supply remaining constant, the equilibrium price rises. More people competing for the same housing stock = higher prices.

2. Home prices continue rising because demand factors (population growth: {{STATE_POPULATION_GROWTH}}, job growth: {{STATE_JOB_GROWTH}}, strong economy) continue to shift demand RIGHT faster than supply can increase. Even at ${{STATE_MEDIAN_HOME_PRICE}}, quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied at current prices, pushing prices higher toward a new equilibrium.

3. Higher summer gas prices are primarily a DEMAND shift. During summer vacation season, demand for gas shifts RIGHT (more travel, road trips, vacations). Supply remains relatively constant. Increased demand + stable supply = higher equilibrium prices. In winter, demand shifts back LEFT and prices fall.

4. Understanding supply and demand helps career planning by identifying fields where demand for workers exceeds supplyŌĆöthese careers offer higher wages and better job security. Look for careers with: (1) High demand due to trends (aging population ŌåÆ healthcare, digital transformation ŌåÆ tech), (2) Limited supply (skilled trades with retiring workers), (3) Strong {{STATE_NAME}}-specific demand ({{STATE_UNEMPLOYMENT_RATE}}% unemployment indicates tight labor market). Choose careers where you can develop skills that are in high demand but limited supply.
